title: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)

lines.text: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)

Core idea: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) — wipe out a patient's bone marrow, infuse new hematopoietic stem cells (donor or own, edited or unedited), let them home, engraft, and rebuild the entire blood and immune system.
The chassis for modern cellular medicine.
The HSC: hematopoietic stem cell.
Self-renewing, multipotent, lives in marrow niches, expresses CXCR4 to follow CXCL12 chemokine gradient home from the bloodstream.
Roughly 10-20K long-term HSCs per adult; produces ~100B blood cells/day downstream.
Founder: E.

Donnall Thomas — first attempts 1956 (Cooperstown), first successful HLA-matched allogeneic BMT 1968-69 (Seattle/Fred Hutchinson); Nobel Prize 1990 (shared with Joseph Murray for solid organ transplant).
HLA matching: MHC class I (HLA-A/B/C, all cells) and class II (HLA-DR/DQ/DP, immune cells).
Typically 10/10 matching across A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1.
Sibling: 25% chance of match.
Registry (Be The Match): ~75% for European ancestry, <25% for African/mixed.
Inequity is the central access problem.
Allogeneic vs autologous vs syngeneic: Allogeneic — donor cells.
Used for leukemias, aplastic anemia, SCID, sickle cell.
Risks: GVHD, graft failure, opportunistic infection.
TRM ~10-30% at 1 year.
Autologous — patient's own.
Used for myeloma, lymphoma (high-dose chemo rescue) and for gene therapy.
No GVHD.
Syngeneic — identical twin.
Rare.

Indications: Leukemias (AML, ALL, CML pre-imatinib, CLL pre-BTKi) Lymphomas (NHL, Hodgkin) — autologous standard for relapsed Multiple myeloma — autologous standard Severe aplastic anemia — allo cure SCID and other primary immunodeficiencies Sickle cell, beta-thalassemia (allo or gene therapy) Storage diseases (ALD, MPS) — gene therapy delivery Autoimmune (SSc, MS, IBD) — experimental "immune reset"
Conditioning: Myeloablative — busulfan + cyclophosphamide (Bu/Cy); Cy + TBI.
Full destruction, more toxicity.
Reduced-intensity (RIC) — fludarabine + melphalan (Flu/Mel); fludarabine + low-dose busulfan.
Relies on graft-vs-tumor.
Non-myeloablative — minimal chemo, max immunosuppression.
For autologous: single high-dose agent (melphalan for myeloma, BEAM for lymphoma, busulfan for gene therapy).
Stem cell sources: Bone marrow harvest — OR under GA, posterior iliac crests, ~1 L collected.
Original method, still used selectively.
Peripheral blood (PBSC) — G-CSF mobilization ± plerixafor, apheresis.
Dominant source today.
Faster engraftment, higher chronic GVHD.
Cord blood — banked at birth (Gluckman 1988).
Naive cells tolerate HLA mismatch (4/6 acceptable), but small dose, slower engraftment, higher graft failure.
Critical for minority patients.
Engraftment: Homing via CXCR4-CXCL12, transendothelial migration into marrow.
ANC > 500 (neutrophil engraftment): PBSC ~day 12-14, marrow ~day 18-21, cord ~day 25-35.
Platelet engraftment > 20K, then red cells.
Full immune reconstitution takes months to years.
Gap period: profound pancytopenia, isolation, prophylactic antimicrobials, transfusion support.
GVHD: Donor T cells attack recipient tissues.
Acute (<100 days) — skin (rash), gut (diarrhea), liver (cholestasis).
Grades I-IV.
Chronic (>100 days, often months-years) — skin scleroderma, dry eye, mouth lesions, lung BO, etc.
Prophylaxis: tacrolimus/cyclosporine + methotrexate (Storb regimen).
PTCy increasingly used.
Treatment: steroids first; ruxolitinib for steroid-refractory; ECP, ibrutinib, etc.
Graft-versus-tumor (GVT): Same alloreactive T cells that cause GVHD also kill residual cancer.
T-cell-depleted grafts → less GVHD, more relapse.
Donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI) exploits GVT directly for relapse post-transplant; classic for CML.
Central therapeutic value of allogeneic HSCT; the conceptual ancestor of modern immunotherapy.
Haploidentical transplant (Baltimore platform): Half-matched family donor (parent, child, half-matched sibling).
Fuchs, Luznik, Jones at Johns Hopkins, early 2000s.
Post-transplant cyclophosphamide (PTCy) on days +3/+4 selectively kills proliferating alloreactive donor T cells.
Transformed access — every patient with a first-degree relative has a donor.
Now also applied to matched/mismatched unrelated donor transplant for GVHD reduction.
Modern conditioning frontier: Anti-CD117 ADCs (Magenta, Jasper) — selective HSC depletion without busulfan.
Anti-CD45 antibodies — broader leukocyte depletion.
Goal: outpatient conditioning, no infertility, dramatically lower TRM.
Key enabler for pediatric gene therapy and population-scale sickle cell cure.
HSCT for sickle cell: Curative since 1980s with matched sibling allogeneic HSCT (~85% cure).
Access barrier: only ~20% have matched sibling, registry coverage poor for African ancestry.
Berlin patient (Timothy Brown, 2007) — HIV cure via CCR5-mutant allo HSCT.
Solved by autologous gene therapy.
Gene therapy on HSCs (all BLA cellular therapies): Casgevy (Vertex/CRISPR, 2023) — CRISPR KO of BCL11A enhancer → fetal hemoglobin reactivation.
Sickle cell, beta-thal.
Lyfgenia (bluebird, 2023) — lentiviral T87Q anti-sickling beta-globin.
Sickle cell.
Zynteglo (bluebird, 2022) — lentiviral functional beta-globin.
Transfusion-dependent beta-thal.
Skysona (bluebird, 2022) — lentiviral ABCD1.
Cerebral adrenoleukodystrophy.
Strimvelis (GSK/San Raffaele, EU 2016) — gamma-retroviral ADA.
ADA-SCID.
All: mobilize CD34+, edit/transduce ex vivo, myeloablative busulfan, reinfuse.
HSCT-gene therapy convergence: Same infrastructure: apheresis, GMP manufacturing, conditioning, infusion, engraftment monitoring.
Same regulatory pathway: FDA/CBER BLA for cellular therapy.
Same clinicians, same wards, same workflows.
Gene therapy = HSCT + editing step.
CAR-T = autologous lymphocyte therapy on transplant rails.
Frontier: Antibody-based conditioning (CD117 ADCs) replacing busulfan.
CAR-HSC for in vivo, durable CAR-T generation.
In vivo HSC editing via AAV6 or LNP, eliminating ex vivo manufacturing.
Cost reduction toward population-scale sickle cell cure (~$2M → manageable).
Allogeneic universal donor HSCs, off-the-shelf cellular products.

Two organizing principles: (1) the hematopoietic stem cell's unique combination of self-renewal, multipotency, and chemokine-driven homing makes IV infusion of stem cells a workable transplant strategy — almost uniquely among adult tissues; (2) HSCT is the chassis, and modern cellular medicine (gene therapy, CAR-T) is a series of new payloads built on that chassis.
Understand the chassis, and the new payloads make sense as variations.
